ideas.intro: Ideas for projects, activities, books, games, and more for students with a high level of interest.

items.content:
[ ] This [National Geographic page](https://education.nationalgeographic.org/resource/maps-and-models/) has information on maps of familiar places along with simple questions to explore those maps. 
[ ] Choose a museum from [this list](https://studenttravelplanningguide.com/virtual-field-trips/) of museums by state and take a virtual field trip. (A parent should preview all links.)
[ ] Follow the [Pony Express](https://nationalponyexpress.org/re-ride/current-reride/) re-ride from St. Joseph, Missouri to Sacramento, California either from this year or previous re-rides. You can also send a letter along on the journey!

[ ] Try a recipe from [one of the states](https://a.co/d/6QqQ4JL) you are studying. Check your library or online for ideas.
[ ] Pick a landmark from one of the states you are learning about and try to recreate it using LEGOs or clay.
[ ] Take a [virtual tour](https://www.nationalparks.org/connect/blog/take-virtual-visit-national-park) or watch a webcam from a National Park.

[ ] Route 66 carried hundreds of thousands of Depression-era migrants from the Midwest who went to California hoping for jobs and a better life. Explore some of the famous stops and consider your own[ road trip](https://www.route66roadtrip.com/).
[ ] [Print ](https://www.nationalgeographic.org/society/learn-with-us/mapmaker-launch-guide/)and assemble a table-sized or floor-sized map of the United States and use it to play simple games such as taking small cars on a road trip or playing Simon Says.
[ ] You can find out what indigenous groups lived in your zip code by entering it [here](https://land.codeforanchorage.org/).
